title: Arkansas Baptists Pass Resolution on Abolishing Abortion at State Convention
author: James Silberman
orientation: Reports on the Arkansas Baptist State Convention's October 2021 passage of a Resolution on Abolishing Abortion, including how Pastor Russell Threet successfully amended the Resolutions Committee's diluted draft to remove the broad life-of-the-mother exception and replace it with narrower language addressing ectopic pregnancies. The article also rebukes pro-life establishment figures who argued for compromise and contrasts that posture with the abolitionist call for immediate, no-exceptions action.
audiences: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to introduce a similar resolution
Pro-life advocate evaluating the no-exceptions versus exceptions debate
Arkansas legislator or state official addressed by the resolution
Skeptical layperson asking about ectopic pregnancy and 'life of the mother' claims
Movement organizer studying tactical wins inside denominational politics

questions.question: What did the Arkansas Baptist State Convention actually resolve in October 2021?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to introduce a similar resolution
questions.answer: The convention adopted a resolution declaring abortion to be murder and calling on Arkansas governing officials at every level to enact legislation that abolishes abortion immediately and without compromise, on the basis of God's Word and the Constitution.
questions.quote: It is now the position of Arkansas Baptists — the largest Christian denomination in the state — that governing officials in Arkansas at all levels must work “with all urgency to enact legislation using the full weight of their office to interpose on behalf of the preborn, abolishing abortion immediately, without compromise.”

questions.question: Who introduced the resolution and what was their connection to the national SBC effort?
questions.audience: Movement organizer studying tactical wins inside denominational politics
questions.answer: Pastors Russell Threet of First Baptist Church of Mena and Dave Hughey of Geyer Springs First Baptist Church submitted it. Both had cosigned the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion that passed at the national SBC meeting in June 2021, and they brought the same language to the Arkansas convention.
questions.quote: Pastors Russell Threet (First Baptist Church of Mena) and Dave Hughey (Geyer Springs First Baptist Church) — both of whom cosigned the resolution that was passed at the national meeting — submitted the same language for consideration at the Arkansas Baptist State Convention’s annual meeting which was held on Tuesday and Wednesday.

questions.question: How did the Resolutions Committee try to weaken the original abolitionist language?
questions.audience: Movement organizer studying tactical wins inside denominational politics
questions.answer: The committee partially accepted the resolution but struck several articles and inserted its own language, including a broad life-of-the-mother exception clause that the abolitionist sponsors had not written.
questions.quote: The Arkansas Baptist Resolutions Committee partially accepted the resolution but amended it by striking a number of the articles and adding some of their own language.

questions.question: How did Threet rewrite the exception clause, and what distinction did he draw?
questions.audience: Skeptical layperson asking about ectopic pregnancy and 'life of the mother' claims
questions.answer: Threet moved to strike the committee's broad exception language and substitute a narrower article specifically addressing ectopic pregnancies, framing them not as abortions but as tragedies requiring medical triage that still treats both mother and baby as human beings.
questions.quote: Resolved, that we recognize that ectopic pregnancies are not murder by abortion but tragedies during which medical triage is necessary, with doctors never ceasing to treat both the mother and baby as human beings, and upholding the Hippocratic Oath by exhausting all possible options to save as many human lives as they can, and be it futher

questions.question: What is the abolitionist response to claims that abortion is sometimes medically necessary to save a mother's life?
questions.audience: Skeptical layperson asking about ectopic pregnancy and 'life of the mother' claims
questions.answer: The article points to thousands of OBGYNs and maternal health professionals, including signers of the Dublin Declaration, who affirm that purposefully destroying a preborn child is never medically necessary to save a mother's life, and warns that the 'life of the mother' clause functions as a loophole the abortion industry exploits.
questions.quote: This life of the mother exception is a massive hole that the industry has utilized to keep murdering… I urge you to pass these amendments for God’s glory, for the comfort of our brothers and sisters in crisis, and the rescue of our preborn neighbors.

questions.question: Why does the article call Arkansas's recent pro-life law a 'non-existent achievement'?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate evaluating the no-exceptions versus exceptions debate
questions.answer: Because the law did not actually criminalize abortion in Arkansas; it merely asked the U.S. Supreme Court to act, leaving mothers free to abort their preborn children with state protection still in place.
questions.quote: It is important to note that the Arkansas legislature did not make abortion illegal in the state. Any mother can murder her preborn child in Arkansas with the protection of the state government. What Arkansas did was pass a law which pathetically “pleads with the United States Supreme Court to do the right thing.”

questions.question: What argument did Larry Page of the Arkansas Faith & Ethics Council give for keeping the committee's softer language?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate evaluating the no-exceptions versus exceptions debate
questions.answer: Page argued that Arkansas Southern Baptists hold differing views on which exceptions (rape, incest, life of the mother, or none) should be allowed, and that the committee aimed for a 'reasonable consensus' rather than a no-exceptions stance.
questions.quote: Arkansas Southern Baptists aren’t monolithic on this issue [of exceptions that allow for some abortion]. Rape, incest, life of the mother, or none. There are folks that fall in those categories. And so, with these weighty considerations, there were seven of us in a room praying and searching diligently for what we thought would be a reasonable consensus of Arkansas Southern Baptists.

questions.question: How does the author respond to the claim that church leaders should accommodate members who favor exceptions?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to introduce a similar resolution
questions.answer: Silberman argues that a leader's job is to guide his sphere of influence away from wickedness, and that the existence of Baptists supporting exceptions is itself a reason to name and repudiate the sin more clearly, not to soften the convention's witness.
questions.quote: It should go without saying that the role of any leader in the Church is to guide those in his sphere of influence away from wickedness, and how much more so for a man who leads the ethics and policy arm of the denomination? That some Baptists support these exceptions is no reason for the convention to avoid speaking clearly to the issue. It is all the more reason to name the sin and repudiate it.

questions.question: What case does Hughey make for using uncompromising language in church resolutions?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to introduce a similar resolution
questions.answer: Hughey argues that almost 49 years of pro-life laws with exceptions have failed to stop abortion, that 2,363 Americans are still aborted daily, and that the church must break the cycle by speaking without compromise rather than depending on regulatory half-measures.
questions.quote: But we need strong language so we don’t stay on this hamster wheel that we’ve been on all these years. With all the pro-life laws that have been passed to regulate abortion, there are still 2,363 Americans aborted every day in our nation.

questions.question: What does the resolution call on Arkansas pastors and entity leaders to do specifically?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to introduce a similar resolution
questions.answer: It calls them to deploy their God-given gifts of preaching, teaching, and leading with one unified, principled, prophetic voice aimed at abolishing abortion in Arkansas.
questions.quote: Resolved, that as Arkansas Baptists, we will engage, with God’s help, in establishing equal justice and protection for the preborn according to the authority of God’s Word as well as local and federal law and call upon pastors and entity leaders to use their God-given gifts of preaching, teaching, and leading with one unified, principled, prophetic voice to abolish abortion.

questions.question: What scriptural foundation does the resolution offer for treating abortion as murder?
questions.audience: Skeptical layperson asking about ectopic pregnancy and 'life of the mother' claims
questions.answer: It grounds the claim in the Noahic covenant's principle of retributive justice and the sixth commandment's prohibition of murder, framing the killing of a preborn image-bearer as ultimately an assault on God's image and an attempt to usurp God's sovereignty as Creator.
questions.quote: Whereas, to murder any preborn image-bearer is a sin, violating both the natural law of retributive justice as set forth in the Noahic covenant, as well as the sixth commandment forbidding murder, and as such, is ultimately an assault on God’s image, seeking to usurp God’s sovereignty as Creator (Genesis 9:5-6; Exodus 20:13; Proverbs 6:17); and

questions.question: What practical follow-through does Hughey say churches owe to women if they take a no-compromise public stance?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate evaluating the no-exceptions versus exceptions debate
questions.answer: Hughey says churches should embody hospitality and grace through action, building cultures of adoption and fostering, supporting crisis pregnancy centers, helping women who regret their abortions, and offering post-abortive ministries.
questions.quote: Our churches need to create a culture of adoption, of fostering; and encourage our people to do that. We need to support crisis pregnancy centers. We need to help women who regret their abortions, and there are many. We need to offer post-abortive ministries for those women.

questions.question: What broader trend does Silberman say this Arkansas vote is part of?
questions.audience: Movement organizer studying tactical wins inside denominational politics
questions.answer: He frames it as part of a wave set in motion by the SBC's June 2021 resolution, with abolitionists in numerous states bringing similar resolutions to their state conventions over the winter and emboldened pastors pushing harder inside the denomination.
questions.quote: When the Southern Baptist Convention passed the Resolution on Abolishing Abortion at their annual meeting in June, a powerful string of events was set in motion as longtime abolitionists became emboldened to push even harder for abolitionism within the convention and hundreds of pastors and SBC messengers who were previously unaware of the Abolitionist Movement joined the cause.

questions.question: What does the article want readers in Arkansas to do after reading?
questions.audience: Movement organizer studying tactical wins inside denominational politics
questions.answer: It directs Arkansans to plug into Free the States and to also connect with Abolish Abortion Arkansas through their website and Facebook page so the resolution's mandate translates into local activism.
questions.quote: If you live in Arkansas, make sure to also connect with Abolish Abortion Arkansas at their website and Facebook page.
